title: 5.1 and 5.2 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 5.1 and 5.2!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why the Persian Empire collapsed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The empire faced significant internal unrest before Alexander the Great’s invasion.
Alexander the Great spent decades weakening the Persian Empire through military campaigns.
Deforestation and climate issues led to food shortages and social collapse.
Darius III was unable to stop Alexander the Great’s army.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two factors most determine whether an empire can survive internal or external threats?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: cost of maintaining the empire and how united its people are
number of trade routes and strength of its army
personal wealth of the emperor and size of the capital city
power of its navy and control over religious institutions

assessment_items.question_content:
**What new evidence has caused historians to rethink earlier explanations for the collapse of the Maya during the Classical Period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Researchers found that Maya farmers practiced forest conservation when expanding farmland.
Newly translated texts showed that Maya cities fell due to internal religious disagreements.
Evidence of sudden mass migration to northern Mexico during the Classical Period was uncovered.
Scientists discovered that the Maya had no system for storing water, making drought the most likely cause.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a shared cause behind the fall of both the Inca and Persian Empires?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both empires were weakened by internal instability before facing external conquest.
Both empires collapsed due to widespread famine and environmental disaster.
Both empires voluntarily surrendered to invading forces after diplomatic talks failed.
Both empires fell after lengthy wars with neighboring rival empires.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How can changes or imbalances in population negatively affect the strength of an empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Sudden drops or rapid growth in population can strain resources, disrupt the economy, or weaken the military.
A smaller population means there will be fewer people practicing the empire’s religion.
Inconsistent populations usually lead to more rainfall and climate instability.
Population changes mostly affected the emperor’s mood and had little impact on the empire itself.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the concept of *romanitas* (Roman identity) have both positive and negative effects on the Roman Empire’s unity?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It fostered a shared identity and unity among Roman citizens, but it excluded non-Romans, creating resentment.
It strengthened the empire’s economy but weakened its religious institutions.
It strengthened educational institutions, but maintaining them placed a heavy financial burden on the empire.
It encouraged diverse groups to migrate into the empire, but the government struggled to manage the growing population.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did Roman traditions outlive the fall of the Western Roman Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Roman institutions continued, and the Germanic kingdoms modeled themselves after Rome.
The Germanic tribes that took over preserved a centralized Roman-style government.
The Byzantine Empire kept practicing the same form of Christianity as the Western Roman Empire.
Trade networks across the former Roman world remained strong and uninterrupted.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was the Eastern Roman Empire able to outlive the Western Roman Empire by nearly a thousand years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Eastern Roman Empire had greater cohesion and stronger finances.
The Eastern Empire was too far away for the Germanic tribes to pose a serious threat.
The Eastern Empire had better leadership than the West.
The Eastern Roman Empire had a more centralized authority.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Han emperors’ claim to divine authority contribute to the collapse of the Han Dynasty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People blamed them when natural disasters struck, believing it showed they had lost the Mandate of Heaven.
The emperors ignored natural disasters, focusing only on religious rituals.
The belief in divine authority led people to believe disasters were punishment from foreign invaders.
Because of divine authority, emperors could prevent natural disasters but failed due to poor leadership.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following was an unintended consequence of the Han Dynasty’s defeat of the Xiongnu?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The removal of the Xiongnu as a buffer left the Han Empire more exposed to attacks from other nomadic tribes.
The victory created unity within the Han forces, which strengthened the military and the empire overall.
Xiongnu farmers formed the tax base, so their absence drained the empire’s resources and weakened the empire.
The defeat of the Xiongnu led directly to peaceful unification with all neighboring nomadic peoples.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the influence of eunuchs and child emperors contribute to the fall of the Han Dynasty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Powerful eunuchs manipulated young emperors, creating instability and conflict.
Eunuchs and child emperors strengthened central control by promoting Confucian officials.
The presence of eunuchs ensured loyalty to the emperor, helping unify the empire.
Young emperors created peace by avoiding conflict, while eunuchs focused on economic reform.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The rulers neglected the welfare of the people and entrusted state affairs to favorites and flatterers. Taxes became unbearable, lands were seized by the rich, and the poor had nowhere to turn. Rebellions flared up, and the empire crumbled from within.

>—Fan Ye, *Book of the Later Han,* 500 CE


**Based on the passage, which of the following best reflects a major cause of the Han Dynasty’s fall?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The central government failed to protect the people’s interests, leading to inequality and revolt.
The Han emperors focused too much on expanding the empire rather than governing it.
The emperor was overthrown by invading nomadic tribes who resented Han rule.
Widespread support for eunuch rule caused instability among noble families.

assessment_items.question_content:
>With the Yellow Turbans rising in every province, the court was paralyzed. The generals sent to suppress them raised private armies and began seizing land for themselves. Order broke down, and the authority of the emperor became an illusion.  

>—Zuo Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals (compiled later, reflects early Han historical perspectives)


**According to the excerpt, how did the Yellow Turban Rebellion contribute to the collapse of the Han Dynasty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led military leaders to build personal power, undermining the central government.
It caused provinces to declare independence and form new dynasties immediately.
It restored confidence in the emperor’s leadership after the rebellion was crushed.
It unified the Han elite around a common enemy, preserving the empire.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Rome is fallen, but life continues. We pay new rulers and attend the same markets. The Church now guides our morals where once the Senate did. Perhaps the empire is gone, but something still stands.   

>—Roman merchant letter, late 5th century CE


**What does this passage suggest about some Romans’ perspectives on life after the fall of the Western Roman Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They adapted to the new political realities and saw elements of Roman life continuing.
They believed all aspects of Roman life and culture had completely vanished.
They felt the fall marked the end of civilization and the start of chaos.
They rejected the authority of the Church and longed for the return of the Senate.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Now we are governed by men who care only for gold and pleasure. They do not protect the frontiers, nor do they care for the people. Each thinks only of himself and forgets the empire.  

>—Anonymous Roman observer


**What internal weakness does the author blame for the decline of the Roman Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The author blames weak and selfish leaders that neglected the needs of the empire.
The author believes the rise of Christianity and decline of traditional Roman religion are at fault.
The author argues that an increase in attacks from outside groups like the Huns and Goths caused the decline.
The author blames the failure to maintain military discipline among the soldiers.
